title: History of Python
description: Python was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991. The language emphasizes readability, simplicity, and explicitness — famously captured in the Zen of Python.

timeline.title: Project Conception
timeline.event: Guido van Rossum begins implementation of Python at CWI in the Netherlands during the Christmas holidays, aiming for a readable and extensible successor to the ABC programming language.

timeline.title: First Public Release
timeline.event: Python 0.9.0 is posted to alt.sources. It introduced object-oriented programming with classes, inheritance, exception handling, functions, and core datatypes like list, dict, and str.

timeline.title: Python 1.0
timeline.event: First major release. Introduced functional programming tools: lambda, map, filter, and reduce, laying the groundwork for Python's powerful data processing capabilities.

timeline.title: Python 2.0
timeline.event: Introduced list comprehensions (borrowed from Haskell), a cycle-detecting garbage collector to automate memory management, and native Unicode support.

timeline.title: Python 3.0
timeline.event: A major backward-incompatible release designed to clean up design flaws. Key features include the print function, strict Unicode strings by default, and restructured standard libraries.

timeline.title: Python 2.7
timeline.event: The final release of the Python 2.x branch, intended to provide a long-term transition path to Python 3 with backported features like ordered dicts.

timeline.title: Python 2.x EOL
timeline.event: Python 2.7 officially reaches its End of Life (EOL) on January 1, 2020, ending over two decades of support and transitioning the ecosystem fully to Python 3.

timeline.title: Python 3.10
timeline.event: Introduced Structural Pattern Matching (the match-case statement), parenthesized context managers, and significantly improved syntax error reporting.

timeline.title: Python 3.12
timeline.event: Features a highly optimized CPython interpreter, isolated subinterpreters with separate GILs (PEP 684), generic type syntax improvements, and cleaner stack traces.

timeline.title: Python 3.13
timeline.event: Features an experimental JIT compiler for faster execution, an experimental free-threaded mode running without the GIL (PEP 703), and a brand-new interactive REPL.

timeline.title: Python 3.13+ Iteration
timeline.event: Active steering of the ecosystem towards full GIL-free concurrency, further compiler performance optimization, and broader JIT adoption across packages.

details:
Python was conceived in the late 1980s by Guido van Rossum at Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI) in the Netherlands, as a successor to the ABC programming language. Van Rossum sought to address ABC's limitations while preserving its elegance and readability. The name "Python" was chosen not for the snake species but as a tribute to Monty Python's Flying Circus, reflecting the project's emphasis on making programming enjoyable. The language's design philosophy is codified in PEP 20 (The Zen of Python), which enshrines principles of readability, simplicity, and explicitness that continue to guide the language's evolution.

Python's evolution spans several major eras. Python 1.0 (1994) introduced functional programming tools including lambda, map, filter, and reduce. Python 2.0 (2000) brought list comprehensions, a garbage collector, and Unicode support. The landmark Python 3.0 (2008) was a deliberate break from backward compatibility to fix fundamental design flaws — most notably unifying str and unicode types, changing print from a statement to a function, and altering integer division semantics. The transition from Python 2 to 3 took over a decade, with Python 2 officially reaching end-of-life on January 1, 2020. The Python documentation archive provides a comprehensive record of this evolution across all major releases.

Today, Python is one of the most widely used programming languages globally, driven by its dominance in data science, machine learning (TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn), web development (Django, Flask, FastAPI), and automation. The Python Package Index (PyPI) hosts over 500,000 packages, forming one of the largest and most diverse software ecosystems in history. Python's governance transitioned from van Rossum's Benevolent Dictator For Life (BDFL) model to a steering council in 2018, as outlined in PEP 8016, ensuring the language's continued evolution through community-driven decision-making. The animated timeline above visualizes the key milestones in Python's development from conception to the present day.
